Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

Denis Diderot

1713 – 1784

The most coherent reconstruction of Diderot’s philosophy of mind is a vital-materialist, embodied, developmental monism.

Diderot on soul, mind, sensibility, and intelligence

Central thesis

The most coherent reconstruction of Diderot’s philosophy of mind is a vital-materialist, embodied, developmental monism. The phenomena traditionally attributed to the soul—sensation, perception, memory, imagination, judgment, affect, desire, selfhood, and moral orientation—are real phenomena, but they are not the acts of an immaterial Cartesian substance lodged inside the body. They are functions of living, organized, sensitive matter. Diderot’s “materialism” is therefore not simply a doctrine of dead particles in motion. It is a theory of matter as active, self-organizing, physiologically differentiated, and capable—under the right organization—of sensibility, memory, and thought. This is why Charles T. Wolfe’s Stanford account rightly describes Diderot’s matter as self-organizing and endowed with vital properties rather than as merely inert extension.

The “soul” in Diderot should not be read as a term he merely throws away. In his mature texts, he often naturalizes it. Sometimes “soul” names the organism’s unity, sometimes the organized body’s sensitive and cognitive functioning, sometimes the whole animal as a center of memory and action, and sometimes a theological or metaphysical term under dispute. In the Éléments de physiologie, he asks what would remain of the soul if one abstracted from bodily sensation, pain, pleasure, passions, fever, intoxication, fatigue, and rest; his answer is that the soul is nothing without the body, and the action of soul on body is, in effect, the action of one bodily part upon another.

God and angels should be treated as serious boundary-cases in this reconstruction. Diderot’s naturalized account of human and animal mind does not simply erase the older theological taxonomy of minds. In his Explication détaillée du système des connaissances humaines, Diderot still maps human knowledge through the concepts of uncreated infinite intelligence, finite embodied intelligence, and finite bodiless intelligence; this yields the traditional domains of natural theology, angels and demons, and the soul. The philosophical question is therefore not “Did Diderot ignore divine and angelic minds?” He did not. The deeper question is: what positive cognitive content can a philosophy grounded in embodied sensibility give to minds that are uncreated, infinite, or disembodied?

My answer is that Diderot treats divine and angelic minds as limit-concepts: indispensable for understanding eighteenth-century metaphysics, theology, and the inherited vocabulary of spirit, but not knowable by the same physiological route as human and animal mind. God’s mind appears in Diderot as an infinite intelligence, as the possible divine source of thinking matter, as an object of natural-theological dispute, and as a model of pure mathematical abstraction. Angelic minds appear as created finite bodiless intelligences in theological taxonomy, but Diderot’s mature natural philosophy gives us little reason to project human psychology onto them.

1. Why a reconstruction is necessary

Diderot did not write a single treatise called “On Mind” or “On the Soul.” His philosophy of mind has to be reconstructed from letters, dialogues, encyclopedia articles, physiological notes, art criticism, and fictional or semi-fictional thought experiments. This matters methodologically. The Rêve de d’Alembert is a dialogue, not a scholastic summa; the Lettre sur les aveugles dramatizes arguments through Saunderson; the Éléments de physiologie is a late physiological notebook rather than a polished metaphysical treatise. SEP warns that modern disciplinary categories do not map cleanly onto Diderot’s work: his philosophical project crosses literature, natural history, physiology, metaphysics, aesthetics, and moral psychology.

Censorship also matters. Some of Diderot’s most philosophically revealing writings circulated privately or appeared posthumously. The Rêve de d’Alembert, for example, was written in the late 1760s but published only after his death; SEP describes it as central for understanding his metaphysics and biology, and Treccani notes that many of his more audacious writings remained clandestine, manuscript, or posthumous.

So the reconstruction must proceed with three cautions. First, not every character in a dialogue is Diderot’s direct mouthpiece. Second, not every Encyclopédie article is by Diderot, even where it appears under his editorial umbrella. Third, Diderot’s views shift: early deistic and skeptical materials coexist uneasily with later materialist and physiological speculation. That instability is not a defect of the inquiry. It is part of the philosophy.

2. The historical arc: from empiricism to vital-materialist mind

Diderot’s path begins under the pressure of Locke, Shaftesbury, natural religion, and skepticism. SEP describes the early Pensées philosophiques and Promenade du sceptique as involving deism, skepticism, and natural religion, while the Lettre sur les aveugles radicalizes empiricism toward a materialist metaphysics.

The Lettre sur les aveugles is decisive because it attacks the assumption that human metaphysics is neutral. A blind person’s world is not merely a sighted person’s world with one sense missing. It is a different organization of ideas, moral saliences, spatial judgments, and theological evidence. Diderot writes that the blind person forms figures by repeated tactile experience and by memory of sensations at different points; the sighted person combines colored points, while the blind person combines palpable points.

The Lettre sur les sourds et muets then extends the inquiry into language, signs, gesture, and the structure of thought. Diderot argues that sensible objects first strike the senses, that qualities receive names, and that abstract substantives come later; he warns that people mistake such substantives for real beings, with “substance” becoming especially suspect once stripped of its attributes. This is already a philosophy of mind: the mind is not a pure logical machine; it is a sensory, semiotic, historical formation.

The Entretien entre d’Alembert et Diderot and Le Rêve de d’Alembert then give the mature speculative architecture: matter becomes sensitive; sensitive matter becomes organized; organized sensibility becomes memory; memory makes animal unity and self-consciousness possible; comparison and relation generate judgment and reasoning. Finally, the Éléments de physiologie consolidates this into a physiological account of nerves, brain, organs, sleep, memory, pathology, and the soul.

3. Matter, life, and sensibility

Diderot’s materialism begins by rejecting the Cartesian picture of matter as merely inert extension. Matter is not simply a passive stuff moved from outside. It has internal force, organization, chemistry, life, and sensibility. Spanish and Italian reception often emphasizes this point more strongly than standard Anglo summaries: one Spanish philosophical dictionary tradition describes Diderot as attributing sensitivity to all matter, distinguishing latent or inert sensibility from active organic sensibility; Treccani similarly presents him as developing an original vitalism based on the sensibility of matter and irritability of organic fiber.

The central concept is sensibilité. In the Éléments de physiologie, Diderot defines sensibility as a quality of the animal that alerts it to relations with what surrounds it. It is associated with nerves, movement, organic life, and the power of bodily parts to be affected. Sensibility is therefore not just “having subjective experience” in a modern analytic sense. It is a living body’s capacity to be affected, to register relations, to respond, and eventually to integrate those relations into memory and action.

Diderot’s famous transformation scenes make the metaphysical point. In the Entretien, Diderot imagines matter passing through earth, plant, food, digestion, flesh, and finally living human organization. The distance between marble and sentient flesh is bridged not by inserting an immaterial soul, but by transformations of matter through nutrition and organization. The egg example is even sharper: an initially insensible mass, through heat and movement, becomes life, sensibility, memory, consciousness, passions, and thought.

This is not straightforward panpsychism if panpsychism means that every particle is already conscious. A better label is pan-sensibilism or vital materialism. Diderot often speaks as if matter has latent sensibility, but active consciousness requires organization. The French secondary literature captures the tension: Bourdin describes the Rêve as centered on the question whether sensibility is a general and essential quality of matter, while another French article notes Diderot’s hypothesis of sensibility either as a general property of matter or as a product of organization.

The distinction matters. If sensibility is universal, then mind is continuous with matter. If sensibility is produced by organization, then mind is emergent from life. Diderot often oscillates between these formulations. The oscillation is philosophically productive: it lets him avoid both supernatural dualism and crude mechanism.

4. The soul as organized bodily unity

Diderot’s mature view does not simply say “there is no soul.” It says that the explanatory work assigned to the soul must be relocated within organized living matter. In the Éléments, he writes that if the soul exists, it is profoundly subordinate to pain, pleasure, passions, wine, fever, intoxication, fatigue, and rest; if one abstracts from bodily sensation, nothing intelligible remains of the soul.

He also explicitly considers the soul as mobile, extended, sensitive, and composite. This is a direct inversion of the Cartesian soul, which is immaterial, unextended, indivisible, and thinking. Diderot asks why the soul’s relation to the body should not be understood as the relation of one portion of body to another. SEP captures this as Diderot pitting the unity of sensibility against the Cartesian unity of subjectivity: the “soul” is displaced toward brain and body, but mental processes are not eliminated.

The Éléments then goes further. Diderot says that the animal is a whole, one, and that this unity may constitute soul, self, consciousness, and memory. He also states that there are no free intellectual operations, and that the difference between a sensitive and a rational soul is a difference of organization. This gives us a compact formula:

Soul = organized animal unity + sensibility + memory + functional integration.

The metaphysical consequence is that soul is not an indivisible metaphysical atom. It is an achievement of organization. This is why Diderot can also speak of three lives: the life of the whole animal, the life of organs, and the life of molecules. Separated organs may live for a time, and molecular life may persist beyond the death of the whole. The “soul” is therefore not a simple thing separable from the organism; it is the unity of a living plurality.

5. Brain, nerves, and the rejection of a Cartesian “seat”

Diderot’s philosophy of mind is neurological, but not narrowly brain-reductive. The brain is essential, yet it does not think in isolation. In the Éléments, Diderot writes that the brain does not think by itself any more than the eye sees by itself. A person needs objects in order to think, just as the eye needs objects in order to see. The brain, helped by memory, recombines materials from experience and creates fantastic beings, but it remains an organ among organs.

This is crucial. Diderot is not saying that “the brain alone” replaces the soul. He is saying that mental life is distributed across the organism’s relations: sense organs, nerves, memory, objects, movements, signs, passions, and bodily states. The brain is a coordinating organ, not a ghostly metaphysical center.

The Encyclopédie article “Âme” contains a useful episode in this history of localization. The entry discusses Descartes’s pineal gland hypothesis and then reports cases in which the pineal gland was absent, obliterated, or putrefied without loss of reason and senses; the article concludes that the soul has been dislodged from the place Descartes assigned it. Because the article “Âme” is composite—partly by abbé Yvon, with a Diderot intervention—one must be cautious in attribution. Crampe-Casnabet’s study emphasizes that the Encyclopédie articles on the soul are not unified: Yvon’s article stresses the theological character of the soul, while Diderot’s contribution raises the reciprocal action of soul and body and the question whether the soul is material.

The mature Diderotian answer is neither pineal dualism nor simple localization. The best model is a nervous network, with a common center or origin that coordinates impressions without becoming an immaterial soul.

6. Perception: each sensory organization has its own world

The Lettre sur les aveugles is one of the great eighteenth-century texts on embodied cognition. Diderot’s blind thinker does not merely lack visual images; he has a different metaphysics. The blind person’s ideas are molded by touch, and Diderot even imagines that a blind and deaf philosopher, if constructing a Cartesian man, would place the soul at the fingertips.

This is not a joke. It is a serious attack on the assumption that we know the “seat” or structure of thought independently of sensory organization. If our most vivid experiences come through vision, we imagine mind as centered in the head and eyes. If our world comes through touch, the fingertips become the privileged site of world-disclosure. Diderot is therefore developing a perspectival epistemology of mind: different bodily organizations disclose different worlds.

He also radicalizes the Molyneux problem. A person born blind who later sees cannot immediately coordinate the visible cube and the tangible cube without experience. Diderot stresses that only experience can teach whether there is conformity between the relations of sight and touch; the eye may need to learn to see, as the tongue learns to speak.

The moral consequences are equally striking. Diderot writes that the morality and metaphysics of the blind differ from ours, and that a being with one more sense than we have might find our morality imperfect. This makes moral psychology dependent on sensory embodiment. Compassion, distance, salience, and value are not pure acts of reason. They depend on how things affect us.

7. Memory, consciousness, and the self

Diderot’s theory of selfhood is one of the strongest parts of his philosophy of mind. The self is not a simple substance. It is a temporally extended unity produced by memory.

In the Entretien, Diderot gives the image of the sensitive harpsichord. A merely sensitive instrument would have momentary consciousness of sound; an animal is such an instrument endowed with memory. Memory binds discrete vibrations into a melody, and we are instruments endowed with sensibility and memory whose senses are played by nature.

The self, then, is not bare sensation. It is sensation retained, ordered, compared, and referred to a continuing history. Diderot says that self-consciousness is consciousness of having been oneself from the first moment of reflection to the present, and that this is founded on memory. Without memory, there is no stable “him” or “self.”

The Rêve develops this into the spider-and-web model. The common origin of the nervous network receives reports of pleasure and pain; the constant relation of impressions to that common origin constitutes animal unity. Memory of successive impressions makes the life-history of the animal and its “I”; memory plus comparison produces thought and reasoning.

The Éléments confirms the same view: after deep sleep or profound distraction, recollection gives us back to ourselves; continuous sensations without memory would yield interrupted consciousness of existence but not consciousness of self.

This is a materialist memory theory of personal identity, but it is not merely Lockean. Locke links personal identity to consciousness and memory; Diderot gives that memory a physiological base in organic continuity, nervous structure, and bodily vicissitude. SEP notes that for Diderot, memory ensures diachronic identity amid the constant turnover of bodily molecules.

8. Thought, judgment, and relations

Diderot does not reduce thought to raw sensation. Thought arises when sensations become perceptions, perceptions are retained by memory, and remembered perceptions are compared. Judgment is the perception of relations.

This appears in several places. In the Éléments, Diderot states that perception comes from sensation, then reflection and judgment follow; he also says that thoughts and intellectual operations are chained, not free-floating acts of an immaterial will. SEP similarly emphasizes Diderot’s theory of judgment as perception of relations and locates his aesthetics and subjectivity inside his broader naturalism.

The Lettre sur les sourds et muets adds an important point: reasoning requires several perceptions to be present to the mind at once. If there were no simultaneous plurality of perceptions, discourse and reasoning would be impossible, because comparison requires co-presence. This makes Diderot’s mind neither a simple stream nor a static substance. It is a field of simultaneous, overlapping, retained, and compared relations.

This also explains why Diderot is so interested in art, gesture, music, and poetic “hieroglyphs.” Thought is often richer and more simultaneous than speech. Language must unfold linearly, while mental life can contain multiple perceptions at once. Diderot writes that if sensation had twenty mouths, it would express many ideas simultaneously; discourse is a cold copy of what happens in the understanding.

9. Language and signs: the mind is semiotic, not merely sensory

Diderot’s theory of mind must include language. Language does not merely label pre-existing ideas; it shapes abstraction, communication, memory, and social cognition.

In the Lettre sur les sourds et muets, Diderot argues that sensible objects first strike the senses, then qualities are named, and abstract terms arise later. The danger is that grammatical substantives are mistaken for real beings. This is why “substance” becomes suspicious: once stripped of attributes, it may be an imaginary entity generated by language.

He also treats gesture as philosophically important. Gesture can reveal the order of thought differently from spoken language, and poetic or musical signs can condense affective and perceptual relations in ways ordinary discourse cannot.

This gives Diderot a layered theory of cognition:

First, bodies affect senses. Second, sensations become remembered and compared. Third, signs stabilize and communicate ideas. Fourth, language makes abstraction possible. Fifth, language can also mislead the mind by reifying abstractions.

That is a remarkably complete philosophy of mind and language for a thinker usually remembered as an encyclopedist, novelist, or materialist provocateur.

10. Imagination, dream, and pathology

Diderot’s imagination is material, but not trivial. The brain, aided by memory, recombines past impressions and produces fantastic beings. The imaginary is not supernatural; it is the recomposition of experiential materials.

Dreaming reveals the same physiology under altered conditions. In the Rêve, sleep is a state in which the ensemble, concert, and subordination of the nervous network loosen. In waking life, the network obeys external objects; in sleep, its own sensibility exercises itself, yielding vivid but disordered sequences.

This gives Diderot a naturalistic account of hallucination, dream, delirium, and perhaps genius. The mind is not false because it is bodily. Rather, its bodily conditions explain both order and disorder. When the network is coordinated by objects, habits, signs, and memory, thought is stable. When coordination breaks down, the same sensibility generates dreamlike or pathological combinations.

This also explains Diderot’s medical ambition. In the Éléments, he writes that since all sensation and affection are corporeal, physical medicine applies to body and soul alike. Yet he immediately adds that such medicine is almost impracticable, because it would require mastery of physiology down to organs, correspondences, and elementary molecules. This is not crude reductionism. It is an ambitious psychosomatic medicine constrained by epistemic humility.

11. Animals and the continuum of minds

Diderot decisively rejects the Cartesian animal-machine doctrine. In the Entretien, the bird emerging from the egg has affections and actions; if one insists that the bird is only an imitative machine, then one risks making the human being a machine in the same sense. The more reasonable conclusion is that the difference between human and animal is a difference of organization.

The Éléments says the difference between sensitive and rational soul is organization. That does not mean Diderot erases all difference between human and animal minds. Humans have more complex memory, language, abstraction, social institutions, and arts. But these are organizational and developmental differences, not evidence of an entirely separate immaterial substance.

Diderot’s animal psychology is therefore continuous but graded. Minimal mind begins in sensibility; animal mind requires integrated sensation and memory; human mind adds more complex comparison, signs, abstraction, moral life, and social reflexivity.

12. Agency, determinism, and moral psychology

Diderot denies libertarian free will in the intellectual sphere. The Éléments says there are no free intellectual operations, and that perceptions, reflections, judgments, and thoughts are chained. SEP likewise characterizes him as committed to causality and determinism, while noting the tension this creates with agency and praxis.

But Diderot is not a fatalist in the sense of passivity. Organization matters; education matters; bodily constitution matters; social relations matter; medicine and politics matter. To say that thought is caused is not to say that all causes are fixed or useless. On the contrary, if minds are bodily and social formations, then they can be changed by diet, education, signs, institutions, medicine, art, and law.

His moral psychology is therefore naturalistic. SEP notes that Diderot grounds ethics in human and species constitution rather than in libertine egoism; he speaks of natural or “animal” dispositions toward goodness and wickedness. Moral life is not outside nature. It is the normative organization of a social animal.

13. God’s mind: not a distraction, but a boundary problem

Diderot’s reflections on God are central to his philosophy of mind because they test whether thought must be embodied, whether matter can think, and whether order in nature implies a supreme intelligence.

In the Explication détaillée, Diderot’s classificatory system distinguishes an uncreated infinite intelligence, a created finite intelligence united to a body, and a created finite intelligence without a body. From these arise the science of God, the doctrine of angels and demons, and the science of the soul. This is not yet the mature materialist Diderot of the Rêve, but it shows that Diderot’s philosophical map includes divine and nonhuman intelligences from the beginning.

The Lettre sur les aveugles then subjects natural theology to a sensory critique. Saunderson tells the minister that if he is to believe in God, God must be made available to his mode of evidence—he says, in effect, “make me touch him.” He grants that animal mechanism may be admirable, but asks what such mechanism has to do with a sovereignly intelligent being. He warns against cutting the knot of ignorance by introducing a being that becomes an even harder knot.

This is not a shallow dismissal of God. It is an epistemological demand: evidence for divine intelligence cannot simply presume the sensory world of the sighted. Diderot also gives the scene tragic dignity. Saunderson finally cries out to the God of Clarke and Newton, and the narrator observes that the arguments he had made were not enough to reassure him. The scene is therefore neither simple atheistic ridicule nor orthodox piety. It is a drama of evidence, embodiment, metaphysical uncertainty, and existential need.

In the Rêve de d’Alembert, Bordeu says that admitting or denying a supreme intelligence depends on questions about matter, eternity, the distinction of substances, human nature, and the production of animals. That is an extraordinarily important statement. God’s mind is not an external theological ornament. It is bound to the whole architecture of Diderot’s philosophy of nature and mind.

The Encyclopédie debate around Locke gives the issue another form: can God give thought to matter? The article reports Locke’s claim that we may never know whether a purely material being can think, and then presses the theological point that it may be impious to deny the Creator the power to give thought and feeling to matter. Here divine omnipotence is used not against materialism, but as an argument against dogmatic anti-materialism. If God can make matter think, then thinking matter is not metaphysically incoherent.

There is also a subtler model of divine cognition in the Lettre sur les aveugles. Diderot imagines a kind of abstraction so high that everything—points, lines, surfaces, solids, thoughts, ideas, sensations, actual things, possible things—could be represented by numerical units. He says this way of philosophizing is too far above us and too close to that of the Supreme Being, who “geometrizes” in the universe.

Thus Diderot’s God, when treated as mind, has at least four roles:

God is the uncreated infinite intelligence of the inherited metaphysical taxonomy. God is the possible creator who could give thought to matter. God is the disputed explanatory ground of natural order. God is the ideal limit of pure abstract cognition, beyond ordinary embodied human thought.

This is a serious philosophy-of-mind function. God’s mind marks the boundary between embodied finite cognition and a possible infinite intelligence whose mode of knowing may not be sensory, discursive, or physiological.

14. Angelic minds and “pure intelligences”

Angelic minds are less developed in Diderot’s own mature physiological texts than human and animal minds, but they are not irrelevant. They appear in the inherited taxonomy of spirit and in the Encyclopédie’s theological and metaphysical vocabulary.

The Diderot-authored Explication détaillée defines a created finite intelligence not joined to a body as the concept that leads to the general notion of spirit; the resulting science includes the doctrine of good and evil spirits, angels and demons. The Encyclopédie article “Esprit,” not safely attributable to Diderot as doctrine, states the traditional Christian classification: God, angels, and the human spirit are the three kinds of spirits; angels are finite thinking beings not joined to bodies, while human souls are finite thinking beings joined to bodies. It also says God and angels are supposed to see things intuitively, without deduction or reasoning.

The article “Ange,” again to be handled as Encyclopédie theology rather than Diderot’s personal doctrine, defines an angel as a spiritual and intelligent substance, first in dignity among creatures, and notes that “angel” originally names an office—messenger or envoy—before common usage attaches to it the idea of an incorporeal, intelligent nature superior to the human soul but created and inferior to God. It also reports disagreement among early Christian authorities over whether angels have subtle bodies or are purely spiritual.

This last point is philosophically important. Angelic mind does not automatically settle the body–mind problem. If angels have subtle bodies, then they may not be pure counterexamples to embodied cognition. If they are bodiless finite intelligences, then they mark a supernatural category of mind that Diderot’s physiological account cannot explain by the same method.

A late Encyclopédie article on “Intelligence,” which Marie Leca-Tsiomis argues can be attributed to Diderot, is especially revealing. Diderot’s examples include ordinary human intelligence, an infinite intelligence announced by the harmony of relations, and “celestial intelligences” in Milton’s poetic scene. Leca-Tsiomis notes that Diderot rewrites Trévoux examples in ways that reduce dogmatic theology: “God” is displaced by “man” in one example, finalistic “order” gives way to “relations,” omnipotence disappears, and angels enter through poetic reference to Milton rather than doctrinal assertion.

A respectful reconstruction should therefore say this: angelic minds remain conceptually important for Diderot because they pose the problem of finite intelligence without organic embodiment. But Diderot’s own positive philosophy of mind gives its richest explanations only where there are bodies, organs, nerves, memory, sensibility, signs, and social relations. Angelic minds belong to theology, metaphysical taxonomy, or poetry; they are not objects of Diderot’s physiology. That does not make them distractions. It makes them limit-cases that reveal the scope and limits of his naturalism.

15. Non-Anglophone reception: useful emphases

The Francophone, Italian, and Spanish materials are helpful because they often foreground dimensions underemphasized in narrower Anglo “materialism” summaries.

French scholarship is especially attentive to the internal tensions of Diderot’s materialism. Bourdin treats the Rêve as perhaps the essential text of Diderot’s philosophy and stresses its central question: whether sensibility is a general and essential quality of matter. French work on the biological rooting of thought emphasizes Diderot’s continuity between inert matter, living matter, animal, and human, while also noting that modern biology cannot simply retain Diderot’s hypotheses of spontaneous generation or universal sensibility.

Italian reception, represented by Treccani and Paolo Quintili, usefully emphasizes the organism as a federation of living parts and the emergence of selfhood. Treccani states that Diderot’s living body is like a federation of organs, each with its own life and sensibility, and that consciousness and reason are the final products of the same complex physical-chemical process that constructs the whole living body. Quintili highlights Diderot’s importance for a “pragmatics of subjectivity” and his striking use of le soi—the self—as an intimate corporeal self formed from a sensitive point into a nervous-organic unity.

Spanish sources often present Diderot through the lens of vitalist or dialectical materialism. One Spanish account emphasizes his attempt to overcome mechanism by configuring a vitalist materialism in which matter can generate natural forms and account for constant movement; another, older Marxist dictionary tradition stresses universal sensibility, latent versus active sensitivity, and thought as a developed form of material sensibility. These sources should be used critically, especially where they impose later Marxist categories, but they correctly draw attention to Diderot’s refusal of a merely inert, mechanical matter.

The convergence across languages is striking: Diderot’s philosophy of mind is best read not as “materialism equals denial of mind,” but as a theory of living matter becoming mind through organization.

16. A systematic Diderotian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Diderot can be stated as the following propositions. 1. There is no absolute ontological gulf between matter and mind. Matter, when organized in living bodies, can become sensitive, remembering, affective, and thinking. Diderot’s examples of nutrition, the egg, and organic development are all designed to erode the gap between inert matter and consciousness. 2. Sensibility is the root of mind. Sensibility is the capacity of living matter to be affected and to register relations. It is not yet full thought, but without it there is no perception, memory, or self. 3. Organization transforms sensibility into mind. The difference between sensitive life and rational thought is not a difference of substance but of organization. 4. The mind is embodied and distributed. The brain is crucial, but it does not think alone. It depends on organs, nerves, objects, memory, and bodily states. 5. The soul is the unity of the living animal, not a separable simple substance. If the word “soul” is retained, it should name the integrated sensitive, remembering, active unity of the organism. 6. Consciousness is not identical with self-consciousness. A being may have sensations, but selfhood requires memory of successive impressions and their relation to a common center. 7. Memory constitutes personal identity. The self is the remembered continuity of impressions, actions, and relations. Without memory, the “I” disintegrates into interrupted consciousness. 8. Judgment is the perception of relations. Thought is not bare sensation; it is comparison, relation, and ordering among retained perceptions. 9. Language both enables and distorts thought. Signs allow memory, communication, abstraction, and social cognition, but they also turn abstractions into fictitious substances. 10. Animal minds differ by degree and organization, not by metaphysical kind. Animals have sensibility, memory, affections, and actions; human distinctiveness lies in organization, language, and social development. 11. Free will, in the libertarian sense, is rejected. Intellectual operations are chained by sensation, memory, organization, passion, and circumstance. Yet because causes can be modified, education, medicine, art, and politics remain meaningful. 12. Divine and angelic minds are limit-concepts. God as infinite intelligence and angels as finite bodiless intelligences belong to the inherited metaphysical-theological map, but Diderot’s positive science of mind applies most fully to embodied living beings.

17. Major tensions and unresolved questions

The first tension is whether sensibility is a universal property of matter or a product of organization. Diderot says things that support both readings. This is not a minor inconsistency; it is the hinge between pan-sensibilism and emergentism. SEP notes that Diderot’s move from inert to active sensitivity is speculative rather than experimentally grounded.

The second tension is between centralization and plurality. Diderot needs a common center to explain selfhood, but he also insists on the lives of organs and molecules. His organism is both one and many. The “soul” is thus a unity of coordination, not a metaphysical simple.

The third tension is between determinism and agency. Diderot’s causal chaining of thought undermines free will, but his practical philosophy still depends on transformation: education, medicine, politics, social institutions, and art can change the causes that shape persons. SEP notes this tension between determinism and Diderot’s concern with agency and praxis.

The fourth tension concerns God and angels. If God and angels are genuine minds, then Diderot’s embodied account cannot be the whole philosophy of mind. If they are theological or poetic limit-concepts, then his physiological theory can remain the core account for natural beings. Diderot does not fully resolve this. Instead, he repeatedly uses divine and angelic intelligence to test the reach of human reason, the possibility of thinking matter, and the legitimacy of natural theology.

Conclusion

Diderot’s philosophy of mind is one of the eighteenth century’s most ambitious attempts to think mind without Cartesian dualism and without reducing life to dead mechanism. It is a philosophy of sensitive matter, organized life, memory-based selfhood, embodied perception, semiotic cognition, affective physiology, and graded animal intelligence.

The soul is not simply abolished. It is reinterpreted as the living unity of an organized body. The mind is not a ghost. It is the activity of a sensitive organism in relation to its world. The self is not a metaphysical atom. It is the remembered history of impressions referred to a common organic center. Thought is not pure spontaneity. It is the comparison of relations within a bodily, linguistic, social animal.

God and angels remain philosophically significant because they mark the limits of this naturalization. God’s mind appears as infinite intelligence, as possible creator of thinking matter, as the disputed source of order, and as the ideal of pure abstraction. Angelic minds appear as created finite bodiless intelligences, important within the inherited taxonomy of spirit but not physiologically knowable. A rigorous Diderotian philosophy of mind should therefore neither dismiss theology nor collapse into it. It should say: Diderot gives us a powerful natural philosophy of embodied minds, while divine and angelic minds remain serious boundary-cases that clarify what his theory can and cannot claim.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
